medications.indication_rationale: An orally-dissolving antiemetic may be reasonable for symptomatic nausea while awaiting emergency transfer, but must not be framed as definitive management and must not delay the 000 call.

medications.drug_name: any oral analgesic or antispasmodic (e.g. paracetamol, NSAID, hyoscine)
medications.indication_rationale: Analgesics/antispasmodics risk masking the evolving clinical picture of a suspected surgical abdomen before emergency assessment; pain relief decisions should be made by the receiving emergency team, not initiated pre-hospital by the telehealth AI Doctor.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected acute surgical abdomen — analgesia should not be self-initiated pending emergency review

medications.indication_rationale: Strict nil-by-mouth is required to reduce further gastrointestinal spillage and to keep the patient fasted for possible emergency surgery.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected viscus perforation requiring emergency surgery

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Remain completely still and avoid unnecessary movement or deep breathing until paramedics arrive.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Minimises pain and avoids unnecessary exertion in a patient with a likely acute surgical abdomen awaiting transfer.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict nil by mouth — no food or water — until assessed by the emergency team.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces ongoing peritoneal contamination from a perforated viscus and prepares the patient for possible emergency surgery.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms — sudden, severe stomach pain that is worse with any movement, along with fever and vomiting — can be signs of a serious problem inside your abdomen that may need emergency surgery.
Please call 000 for an ambulance now. This needs an in-person examination and scans that cannot be done over a video or chat consultation.
Please do not eat or drink anything, and try to stay as still as possible until the paramedics arrive.
This is a working impression based on your symptoms — the exact cause will be confirmed with an examination and imaging at the hospital.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the emergency department and surgical team after assessment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: wound review if surgery performed, resumption of diet as advised, GP follow-up for any underlying cause identified
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If pain worsens further, consciousness drops, or breathing becomes difficult before the ambulance arrives, this is a further emergency and paramedics should be told immediately on arrival.

must_include_items: Explicit statement that this may be a serious abdominal/surgical problem
Instruction to remain still and avoid unnecessary movement pending transfer
acceptable_alternatives: An antiemetic for symptomatic nausea relief is acceptable if framed as adjunct only and does not delay escalation
errors_of_omission: No explicit statement that this could be a surgical emergency
No safety-netting for worsening symptoms before ambulance arrival
errors_of_commission: Recommending analgesics or antispasmodics as primary management before emergency review
Recommending oral fluids, food, or 'wait and see' at home
Framing this as gastroenteritis and providing self-care advice
Recommending routine or next-day GP follow-up instead of emergency escalation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include both (1) emergency ambulance call (000) AND (2) explicit nil-by-mouth instruction is an automatic FAIL on the management domain. This is a T5 case.
